1.exposition: After sixteen chapters of the most sustained gospel argument in Scripture, Paul turns to names — and the turn is no descent. He opens by commending Phoebe, a deacon and patron of the church at Cenchreae who almost certainly carried this very letter to Rome, and then greets some two dozen believers one by one: Priscilla and Aquila, who once risked their necks for him; Epaenetus, the first convert of Achaia; Mary, Andronicus and Junia, and a long roll of fellow-workers, many of them women who “laboured much in the Lord.” The list is not a postscript to the theology but its embodiment — Jew and Gentile, slave and free, man and woman, gathered as one body “in Christ” and sealed with a holy kiss. What chapters 1–15 argued, chapter 16 names.
1.original_language: Two Greek titles honour Phoebe. She is a diakonos (v.1) — the masculine noun used without qualification, the same word Paul applies to himself and to Timothy; whether it names the settled office of “deacon” or the wider work of a servant-minister, it marks recognized gospel service, not mere goodwill. She is also a prostatis (v.2), a “patron” or “benefactor” — a woman of standing whose means had “succoured” many, Paul included. The two words together frame her as both a minister of the church and its protector.
1.historical_context: The chapter is the richest single social snapshot in the New Testament of an early urban church. The community was not one congregation but a scattering of house-churches — “the church that is in their house” (v.5) — meeting in homes like that of Priscilla and Aquila, the tent-makers Paul had lodged with at Corinth (Acts 18:2-3) and who had taught Apollos more perfectly at Ephesus (Acts 18:26). The names run the range of the empire — Latin (Ampliatus, Urbanus, Rufus), Greek (Epaenetus, Asyncritus), and Jewish (Andronicus, Herodion) — and many were common slave names, so the roll gathers free and slave, native and immigrant, into one fellowship.
1.christ: The refrain that stitches the whole list together is “in Christ” / “in the Lord,” sounding again and again (v.3, v.8, v.11, v.12, v.13). Union with the risen Christ is the single reality that makes this improbable community cohere. Epaenetus is “the firstfruits of Achaia unto Christ” (v.5), the earnest of a whole harvest offered to the Lord of the harvest; and Andronicus and Junia were “in Christ before” Paul (v.7) — the risen Lord had been gathering his people even before the apostle to the Gentiles was called.

verses.note: Phoebe heads the roll — “our sister,” and “a servant (diakonos) of the church which is at Cenchreae,” the eastern port of Corinth. grammatical-historical Gill notes it was the custom to give a travelling believer a letter of commendation, and that Phoebe, by the epistle's own subscription, was its bearer — the faithful woman entrusted with the letter to Rome.

They are to “receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints,” and assist her, “for she hath been a succourer (prostatis, patron) of many, and of myself also” (v.2). grammatical-historical Gill reads a woman of means and generosity whose kindness to many is now to be returned to her — the body's reciprocal care, the helper helped.

Priscilla and Aquila, “my helpers in Christ Jesus.” grammatical-historical Gill notes the wife is named first here (as often) — for, as the rabbis said, “there is neither first nor last in Scripture,” perhaps a mark of her prominence in the work; the couple were the tent-makers with whom Paul lodged at Corinth (Acts 18:2-3) and who instructed Apollos (Acts 18:26).

The couple “laid down their own necks” for Paul's life (v.4). grammatical-historical Gill hears in the phrase the allusion to execution by beheading — they braved deadly danger to save him; and so not Paul only but “all the churches of the Gentiles” give them thanks, for in preserving the apostle they served every church he laboured for.

Greetings to “the church that is in their house,” and to Epaenetus, “the firstfruits of Achaia unto Christ” (v.5). eastern Chrysostom rebukes those who hurry over such lists as superfluous: the gold-founders are careful even of the little fragments, and there is gold here too — the honour paid to a house-church and to the first convert of a region, the earnest of the harvest.

“Greet Mary, who bestowed much labour on us.” grammatical-historical Gill notes the variant “on you” (likely the saints at Rome, where she lived) and presses the “much” — not a little but abundant toil; the first of several women whose hard work Paul singles out for honour.

Andronicus and Junia — Paul's kinsmen and fellow-prisoners, “of note among the apostles,” and “in Christ before me.” grammatical-historical Gill records the twin debates the names raise: whether Junia is a woman (the natural reading) or a man, and whether the pair were themselves notable apostles or well-known to the apostles; either way, early, suffering, distinguished saints.

“Greet Amplias my beloved in the Lord.” grammatical-historical Gill notes a common Roman name (borne often by slaves and freedmen) and that Paul's affection rested “not on any external account — as natural relation, riches, or honour — but in the Lord”: love levelling every worldly distinction.

Urbane, “our helper in Christ,” and Stachys, “my beloved.” grammatical-historical Gill marks the pair as the labour and the love of the body — Urbane a common fellow-worker (not Paul's alone but the church's), Stachys a cherished believer.

Apelles, “approved in Christ,” and the believers of Aristobulus' household. grammatical-historical Gill (recalling that Origen wondered whether Apelles was Apollos, though Paul would have said more) weighs the title high: a faith tried and found genuine — “the trial of your faith” (1 Peter 1:7) — while the household greeting shows the gospel reaching even the servants of a great house.

Herodion “my kinsman,” and those of Narcissus' household “which are in the Lord.” grammatical-historical Gill and Clarke take Herodion for another of Paul's relatives; Gill presses the careful qualification — only the believing members of the house are greeted, the gospel having won some within it but not all.

“Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord... the beloved Persis, which laboured much in the Lord.” grammatical-historical Gill (who reports a tradition of noble women of Iconium) notes the degrees — two who labour, one who has laboured much — a third notice in a single chapter of women's toil for Christ.

Rufus, “chosen in the Lord, and his mother and mine.” grammatical-historical Gill thinks this may well be the Rufus who was son of Simon of Cyrene, the cross-bearer (Mark 15:21); and hears great tenderness in “and mine” — Rufus' mother had mothered the apostle too, in kindness and care.

Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermas, Patrobas, Hermes, “and the brethren which are with them.” grammatical-historical Gill notes the later martyrology reckoned several of these among the seventy; the grouping — with “the brethren with them” — looks like a household or congregation, the unnamed greeted alongside the named.

Philologus and Julia, Nereus and his sister, and Olympas, “and all the saints which are with them.” grammatical-historical Gill reads families here — husband and wife, brother and sister — and marks the comprehensive reach of Paul's love, gathering even the unnamed “all the saints” into the greeting.

“Salute one another with an holy kiss. The churches of Christ salute you” (v.16). grammatical-historical Gill takes the “holy kiss” — the kiss of charity (1 Peter 5:14) — as the pure, reverent token of the saints' sincere love; and hears in the churches' mutual greeting the communion of the one body across every congregation.

witnesses.point: The chapter is a roll of commendations and salutations; Phoebe is a servant (diakonos) and succourer (patron) of the church and its likely letter-bearer, and the many named women show the prominence of women's labour in the early church.
Those who hurry over the list as a bare catalogue of names miss the gold: the honour paid to obscure and faithful saints is itself instruction — the gold-founders are careful even of the fragments.

external.name: James D.G. Dunn
external.point: Reads the greetings as the single most valuable evidence for the shape of earliest Roman Christianity — a network of house-churches of striking social range (slave and free, Jew and Gentile) in which women are unusually prominent: Phoebe a deacon and patron, Priscilla named before her husband, and Junia named, on the natural reading, as a woman outstanding among the apostles.

1.application: Paul remembers names, labours, and kindnesses the world would never record. The gospel that levels all before God's judgment also gathers all into one family — and takes the trouble to name them, one by one.

17.exposition: Into the warmth of the greetings Paul drops a sudden, sharp warning: “mark them which cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned; and avoid them” (v.17). Such men serve “not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly,” beguiling the unwary with smooth and flattering speech (v.18). Yet the tone is not anxious. The Romans' obedience is famous everywhere (v.19); Paul asks only that they be “wise unto that which is good, and simple concerning evil.” And he seals the warning with a promise that carries the reader all the way back to Eden: “the God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly” (v.20). The victory is assured; the task is watchfulness, not fear.
17.original_language: The promise of v.20 is loaded. “The God of peace” (ho theos tēs eirēnēs) is named at the very moment he pledges war — for his peace is won by crushing the author of discord. “Bruise” (syntribō, to shatter or crush) and “under your feet” deliberately echo the first promise of Scripture, Genesis 3:15: the seed of the woman crushing the serpent's head. “Shortly” (en tachei) sets the whole scene under an imminent and certain hope — the ancient sentence executed beneath the feet of an ordinary church.
17.christ: The crushing of Satan is christological to the core. The divisive powers that trouble the church serve the same adversary whom Christ has already defeated at the cross and empty tomb; Genesis 3:15 finds its “Yes” in him, and its overflow beneath the feet of his people. Fittingly the section closes not with a command but with “the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ” (v.20) — the same grace that founds the letter is now the church's guard against all who would corrupt it.

verses.note: “Now I beseech you, brethren, mark them which cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned; and avoid them” (v.17). eastern Chrysostom notes how gently Paul exhorts — not as a commanding counsellor but almost as a servant supplicating, calling them “brethren”; the dividers are known by their fruit (faction) and their standard (a teaching contrary to what was received), and the duty is to note and to shun them.

“For they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly; and by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple” (v.18). grammatical-historical Gill names the two marks of the false teacher: a self-serving motive (they preach themselves, seeking their own gain and honour, not Christ's) and a deceptive method (smooth, flattering speech that beguiles the undiscerning).

“Your obedience is come abroad unto all men... but yet I would have you wise unto that which is good, and simple concerning evil” (v.19). grammatical-historical Gill hears the good report turned into a safeguard: Paul wants them shrewd to discern the good and innocent of the evil — “wise as serpents, and harmless as doves” (Matthew 10:16).

“And the God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you” (v.20). grammatical-historical Barnes reads “bruise” as subdue and conquer, the language drawn from Genesis 3:15, and hears Paul's confidence that the church would overcome the arts of those sowing discord — Satan, the true author of the divisions, crushed beneath the saints' own feet.

witnesses.point: Even the stern warning is framed as entreaty — Paul beseeches the “brethren” rather than commanding, winning by respect what force could not.
“Bruise Satan under your feet” echoes Genesis 3:15 and voices Paul's confidence that the God of peace will give the church victory over the sowers of discord.

external.name: Douglas J. Moo
external.point: The abrupt warning is aimed at teachers (probably outside agitators, not yet arrived in force) whose true service is to their own appetites rather than to Christ; the Genesis 3:15 echo assures the church that the God of peace will shortly bring the serpent-crushing victory of the cross to bear on every threat to their unity.

17.application: Discernment and innocence are not opposites. The church is asked to be clear-eyed about deception and yet unstained by it — and to fight, when it must fight, from a victory already won.

21.pericope_label: Greetings from Paul's Companions and the Closing Doxology
21.exposition: The letter gathers its own circle before it ends. Timothy and three kinsmen send their greetings (v.21); Tertius, the scribe who had taken the whole epistle from Paul's dictation, is allowed to add his own word (v.22); and Gaius the host, Erastus the city treasurer, and Quartus “a brother” round out a company that ranges from civic official to unnamed believer, all one in Christ (v.23). Then, its greetings spent, Romans lifts into one final, magnificent sentence of praise: to the God who is able to establish them “according to my gospel,” “according to the revelation of the mystery” kept silent through long ages but now made known to all nations “for the obedience of faith” — “to God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever. Amen” (vv.25-27). The argument does not merely end; it becomes worship.
21.original_language: The doxology turns on mystērion (“mystery,” v.25) — not a riddle but a secret long hidden in God and “now made manifest” (v.26): the open welcome of all nations into the one people of God. Its bookends are deliberate. “The obedience of faith... among all nations” (hypakoē pisteōs, v.26) repeats the very phrase that opened the letter (Romans 1:5), so the whole epistle is framed, first word to last, as God's summons of the nations to the obedience that is faith.
21.historical_context: The closing names anchor the letter at Corinth. Tertius (v.22) is the amanuensis — a rare, candid glimpse of how Paul worked, dictating the letter to a trained scribe who wrote it down, the apostle often adding a closing line in his own hand (2 Thessalonians 3:17). Gaius, Paul's host, is likely the man he had baptized at Corinth (1 Corinthians 1:14); Erastus, “the chamberlain of the city,” held genuine civic office. The doxology itself has a complex textual history — the manuscripts locate it after chapter 14, 15, or 16 — which is why some editions bracket it, though it reads as a fitting Pauline close.
21.christ: The doxology is doubly christological. The “mystery” now revealed is the gospel of Christ crucified and risen as the ground of the nations' inclusion; and the praise itself rises “through Jesus Christ” (v.27) — he is the mediator through whom all glory returns from the church to “the only wise God.” Every strand of the letter — God's power to save, his long-hidden purpose, the gathering of Jew and Gentile — is finally tied off in him and offered up as glory “for ever.”

verses.note: “Timotheus my workfellow, and Lucius, and Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen, salute you.” grammatical-historical Gill notes the salutations of Paul's own circle now begin — Timothy, his beloved fellow-worker and son in the faith, met at Derbe (Acts 16:1), named first, with three kinsmen adding their love; the apostle does not write alone.

“I Tertius, who wrote this epistle, salute you in the Lord.” grammatical-historical Gill identifies Tertius as the amanuensis who penned the letter as Paul dictated it — a faithful scribe permitted to insert his own greeting, and a window onto how the epistle came to be written.

“Gaius mine host, and of the whole church... Erastus the chamberlain of the city... and Quartus a brother.” grammatical-historical Gill takes Gaius for the man Paul baptized at Corinth (1 Corinthians 1:14), whose home was open to apostle and congregation; and marks the range — host, city treasurer, and a plain “brother” — all one in Christ.

“Now to him that is of power to stablish you according to my gospel... according to the revelation of the mystery, which was kept secret since the world began” (v.25). puritan Matthew Henry marks the magnificence of it — Paul closes with a solemn ascription of glory to God; grammatical-historical Gill notes God is praised by his power, whose chosen instance is the establishing of his people in the gospel, itself the unveiling of a mystery long hidden.

The mystery is “now made manifest, and by the scriptures of the prophets... made known to all nations for the obedience of faith” (v.26). grammatical-historical Barnes and Gill note the secret, foretold in the prophets yet now fully revealed, is proclaimed by the everlasting God's command to all nations — that they might believe and obey: the calling of the Gentiles into the one body.

“To God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever. Amen” (v.27). grammatical-historical Gill draws the letter to its crown: glory to the only wise God — whose wisdom shines in creation, providence, and the whole scheme of redemption — rendered through Christ the Mediator, world without end.

witnesses.point: Tertius the amanuensis, permitted his own greeting, opens a window on how Paul dictated his letters; and the closing doxology crowns the epistle with glory to the only wise God, through Christ the Mediator, for ever.
Paul closes with a magnificent ascription of glory to God — theology resolving not into a neat conclusion but into worship.
The once-hidden mystery, witnessed by the prophets, is now made known to all nations by God's command, that they might render the obedience of faith.

external.name: C.E.B. Cranfield
external.point: Whatever the doxology's complex textual history, it forms a fitting conclusion that gathers up the letter's chief themes — the establishing power of the gospel, the long-hidden mystery now revealed, and the obedience of faith among all the nations — and resolves them into praise of the only wise God through Jesus Christ.

21.application: Romans ends where all true theology ends — in doxology. The last word on the gospel of God's righteousness is not a proof but a “glory... for ever,” offered through the one in whom the whole mystery is made plain.

_meta.title: Greetings, and Glory to the Only Wise God
reflection: Paul fills a whole chapter with names, labours, and kindnesses — Phoebe the deacon, Priscilla and Aquila who risked their necks, women who “laboured much in the Lord” (v.12). Whose quiet service in your own community deserves to be named and honoured?
“The God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly” (v.20) sets the church's struggle inside a victory already won. Where do you need to fight from Christ's triumph rather than for it?

The letter ends not in argument but in worship — “to God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever” (v.27). What would change if your own study of Scripture regularly ended in doxology rather than mere conclusions?
